subject: maths
title: The Village Well
question: A village has one well. Every day, four families each draw the same amount of water from it. The well refills itself at a constant rate overnight. If six families lived in the village instead of four, the well would run dry after exactly 12 days. If only two families lived there, the well would never run dry - in fact it would slowly overflow. Assuming the well starts full, how many days would it last with four families?
modelReasoningPath:
Let C = capacity of the well, r = daily refill rate, d = daily usage per family. With 6 families: daily net loss = 6d − r. Well runs dry in 12 days: 12(6d − r) = C → 72d − 12r = C. The "2 families never run dry" condition means 2d ≤ r. The borderline case - just barely never running dry - is r = 2d. Substituting r = 2d into the first equation: 72d − 12(2d) = C → 72d − 24d = C → C = 48d. With 4 families: daily net loss = 4d − 2d = 2d. Days until empty = 48d ÷ 2d = 36 days.

What to listen for: This is one of the harder questions. Award credit for any structured approach - naming variables, correctly interpreting "never runs dry," or getting partway through. A child who correctly identifies that 2 families is the break-even point is showing excellent insight. ---
